Now I have a good understanding of the tweet. Let me provide a critical analysis of this "Auto-Research for Legal Agents" experiment from Harvey.OK, now I have the full picture. This is Niko Grupen (Head of Applied Research at Harvey, the legal AI company) posting about an experiment they call "Auto-Research for Legal Agents." Here's the gist, followed by a critical take:

**What they did:** They ran agents on 12 internal legal tasks (lease review, complaint drafting, tax memos, etc.), had an LLM judge score the outputs against rubrics, then had a *coding agent* read the failure feedback, hypothesize harness improvements, implement them, and rerun. Scores went from 40.8% average to 87.7%.

**Now, the critical lens:**

**The evaluation is entirely self-referential.** The rubrics are Harvey's, the judge is an LLM, and the benchmark is internal. There's no independent legal expert verification reported here. When your optimization target *and* your evaluator are both LLMs, you risk hill-climbing on LLM-judge preferences rather than actual legal quality. LLM judges are notoriously susceptible to surface-level features like formatting, length, and confident-sounding language.

**"Harness engineering" is doing a lot of heavy lifting.** What they're really describing is automated prompt/tooling optimization — the agent isn't learning law, it's learning to better orchestrate its own scaffolding (file conversion pipelines, structured fact sheets, stop hooks). That's useful engineering, but calling it "agent skill acquisition" is a stretch. The underlying model's legal reasoning hasn't changed at all.

**The 100% claim deserves scrutiny.** One task hit 100% completion — but on *whose* rubric, at *what* granularity? A rubric that checks "did it produce a document in the right format with the right sections addressed" is very different from "would a senior associate sign off on this work product." They don't appear to share the rubrics publicly.

**12 tasks is tiny.** They acknowledge this, but it's worth emphasizing. Legal work has enormous long-tail complexity. Showing improvement on 12 curated benchmark tasks tells you very little about real-world generalization, especially when the optimization loop is specifically tuned to those 12 tasks. This is classic overfitting risk.

**The "humans steer, agents execute" framing is doing PR work.** It sounds reassuring but papers over the hard question: who validates the output? If the agent self-improved its harness 10 iterations deep, can a human reviewer even understand *why* it's producing what it produces? The interpretability gap grows with each optimization step.

**It's fundamentally a marketing post.** Harvey just raised $200M at an $11B valuation. This is a thread on X, not a peer-reviewed paper. There are no ablations, no baselines against competing approaches, no error analysis, and no discussion of failure modes. The Artificial Lawyer write-up uncritically amplifies it.

That said — the *direction* is interesting. The idea that you can auto-generate task-specific agent tooling through an evaluate-and-iterate loop is a legitimate and useful engineering insight. It's just much more modest than the framing suggests. It's closer to "automated CI/CD for agent configs" than to "agents that learn law."

What angle are you most interested in digging into?

Niko Grupen currently serves as the Head of Applied Research and a Research Scientist at Harvey, focusing on AI research since April 2023. Prior to this role, Niko worked at Google within the Brain team from June 2022 to April 2023, also specializing in AI research. Niko's experience includes a position as a Machine Learning Engineer at Apple from October 2016 to September 2018, where responsibilities included developing conversational intelligence, and a Software Engineer Intern role at Apple in the summer of 2015, concentrating on emerging technologies. Notably, Niko designed and developed the first prototype of Auto-Unlock for macOS, iOS, and watchOS.

content.title: In AI-Powered Brand Deal, Harvey Partners with Yet Another Harvey -- You Know, Its Other Namesake | LawSites
content.text:
Harvey says the AI-generated Stewart campaign will launch on April 1, 2026, and will run indefinitely — or at least until someone representing Stewart’s estate uses some other AI product to generate a lawsuit to shut down the campaign.

Following its February news that it had entered into a brand partnership withj Gabriel Macht, who played Harvey Specter in the TV series Suits, the legal AI company Harvey said today that it has entered into another such partnership involving another iconic Harvey — only this time it is using AI to make the partnership possible. This time, the Harvey in question is the six-foot-three-and-a-half-inch invisible white rabbit from the 1950 film Harvey, starring Jimmy Stewart as Elwood P. Dowd, a man whose best friend happens to be a pooka — a benevolent but mischievous Celtic spirit in rabbit form. Having now raised more than $1 billion and with what the company described as “boatloads of cash to burn,” it is describing this partnership as its “most ambitious use of generative AI to date.” Using its proprietary AI technology, Harvey will digitally resurrect actor Jimmy Stewart to serve as the company’s newest brand ambassador.

content.title: Qdrant - Noa Recruitment Newsletter - April 2026
content.text:
Posted by Neil Harvey · 1st April 2026 · Qdrant (pronounced “quadrant”) is an open-source vector database and vector similarity search engine. It’s designed to store, manage, and query high-dimensional vector embedding – the numerical representations that AI models produce when they process text, images, audio, or other data. Posted by Neil Harvey · 1st April 2026 · Qdrant (pronounced “quadrant”) is an open-source vector database and vector similarity search engine. It’s designed to store, manage, and query high-dimensional vector embedding – the numerical representations that AI models produce when they process text, images, audio, or other data. Rather than looking up exact matches like a traditional database, Qdrant finds things that are similar, which is what modern AI applications actually need. It sits at the infrastructure layer of AI-powered products – the component that makes semantic search, recommendation engines, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines actually work at scale. Built in Rust, it’s engineered for performance and production reliability, and can be run locally, self-hosted, or via Qdrant Cloud. Built for vector search from the ground up – unlike traditional databases retrofitted with vector extensions, Qdrant was designed specifically for this use case, giving it an edge i

Vector databases have moved from niche AI research tooling to core infrastructure. As RAG pipelines become the standard pattern for building LLM-powered applications, knowing how to work with a vector store is quickly becoming a baseline expectation for backend and ML engineers. Qdrant in particular is gaining traction because it’s open-source, well-documented, and integrates cleanly with the most widely used AI frameworks – LangChain and LlamaIndex among them. Demand for engineers who understand the full AI stack, including the retrieval layer, is growing, and Qdrant is a strong, transferable skill within that space. Semantic search across internal documentation, knowledge bases, or codebases · RAG pipelines powering LLM chatbots with grounded, up-to-date context · Product recommendation engines based on behavioural or content similarity ...

Most of the conversation around AI infrastructure focuses on the models themselves – which LLM to use, how to fine-tune, how to manage costs. But increasingly, the differentiator in production AI systems isn’t the model; it’s the retrieval layer. Vector databases like Qdrant are the component that allows models to work with your data rather than just their training data, and that distinction matters enormously in enterprise contexts where accuracy, relevance, and data recency are non-negotiable. RAG – retrieval-augmented generation – has become the dominant pattern for deploying LLMs in business applications precisely because it sidesteps the limitations of static training. Rather than embedding all your knowledge into a model (expensive, slow, and inflexible), you store it as searchable vectors and retrieve the relevant pieces at query time.

Qdrant handles that retrieval step, and handles it well. It’s quietly become one of the more important pieces of infrastructure in the modern AI toolkit. For tech businesses and the engineers they hire, this creates a clear signal. Understanding vector search isn’t just an AI specialism anymore – it’s part of the broader backend skill set that production AI demands. Companies building anything on top of an LLM will, at some point, need someone who understands how the retrieval layer works. Even for an AI-native company, it's hard. “Leverage is no longer about how much one organization can produce; it’s found in how much context people, teams, and institutions can coordinate across humans and agents.” · With agentic capabilities evolving rapidly, planning for today’s capabilities can feel uselessly obsolete. However, from our vantage we can see clear and enduring shifts in how AI will affect legal both as consumers of agents and as essential stakeholders in how agents are implemented across organizations. Like in other industries, legal agents will begin to challenge structural conventions in law firms. Firms are deeply hierarchical, using reporting chains between associates and partners to channel the limited resource of legal expertise across vastly complex matters. The more junior parts of this hierarchy are focused on throughput — organizing vast troves of data or executing largely rote tasks.

Despite my parents’ backgrounds, they were completely caught off guard by the capability jump in the past few months. They both have PhDs in computer science. My mom worked at Apple for 30 years and led a large team working on autocorrect, one of the first billion-user applications of language models. My dad was a Stanford professor and worked on many scientific computing methods that later became what neural networks are today. They live in Silicon Valley. They have two sons at Harvey. I have been talking about this technology nonstop. They use ChatGPT every day. And they were still completely blindsided by just how good the coding agents had gotten. If you are not an engineer using these models full time it’s hard to appreciate how big a change is coming. In their defense, my parents are both retired. But the bigger point is that most of the world is about to have the same reaction they did.

We are going through that transformation at Harvey right now. We have built an internal agent system called Spectre (named after a Dota 2 character), and it is starting to autonomously handle more and more engineering work and increasingly, more non-engineering work as well. Much of what it does is no longer triggered by a human prompt. It is triggered by the system monitoring the company and making decisions based on incidents, bug reports, customer feedback, and Slack messages. In practice, Spectre is the beginning of a company world model: a live picture of what is happening inside Harvey and what needs to happen next. Our engineers are now so productive that they are harder to coordinate. The bottlenecks are shifting away from implementation and toward review, prioritization, coordination, and operating design. That is what the new leverage looks like inside an organization: more work can happen than the old coordination structure can absorb.

content.title: How Agentic Search Unlocks Legal Research Intelligence at Harvey
content.text:
Our solution was to build a systematic evaluation process powered by Harvey's in-house legal experts. Applied Legal Researchers (ALRs) and Strategic Business Development Leads (SBDLs) are former practicing lawyers who collaborate closely with ...

Our solution was to build a systematic evaluation process powered by Harvey's in-house legal experts. Applied Legal Researchers (ALRs) and Strategic Business Development Leads (SBDLs) are former practicing lawyers who collaborate closely with Engineering on product development. In partnership with these teams, we developed a data flywheel that continuously improves the system: Query Pattern Mining: After receiving aggregated feedback and support tickets that describe issues, our Legal team analyzes these patterns to understand common failure modes. Expert Query Generation: ALRs and SBDLs create evaluation queries that capture real usage patterns, spanning from straightforward searches to multi-jurisdictional analysis. Rapid Prototyping and Testing: We build the initial prototype quickly then aggressively test it with our legal team. This tight feedback loop helps us surface issues, fast.

Agentic Search brings reasoning to retrieval, enabling Harvey to handle a wide range of tasks, from quick document lookups to comprehensive multi-source legal research. By building a privacy-preserving evaluation system powered by legal experts, we've created a system that continuously improves while still maintaining the data protections fundamental to Harvey. This represents a significant step forward in legal research intelligence, but there's more work ahead. Our team is focused on these key areas: Improving AI quality: Continuing to iterate on tool design, reasoning patterns, and evaluation metrics that capture the nuances of legal research quality. Expanding knowledge coverage: Integrating additional legal knowledge sources and databases to ensure agents can access the full breadth of information lawyers need. This capability helps raise the baseline quality of queries in Harvey Assistant, while also serving as the technical foundation for Deep Research — our most comprehensive mode for multi-source legal analysis. content.text:
For case law research, the team at Harvey envisioned an experience where you could copy/paste a client question into a case law model, and it would answer that question thoroughly and cite all its sources. They tried the obvious techniques first: fine-tuning foundation models via public APIs and building retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems. But they ran into limitations with such a uniquely complex, open-ended use case. “If you just do retrieval, you can answer very simple questions about areas of law that you aren’t really an expert in, but that’s actually not that useful for most attorneys,” Weinberg explained. “With case law research, you’re finding ammo for your argument, and that’s much more difficult to do.” Foundation models were strong at reasoning, but lacked the knowledge required for legal work. So, Harvey decided to partner with OpenAI to build a custom-trained model that would allow them to inject new knowledge, and ways of reasoning about that knowledge, into bas

Harvey partners with OpenAI to build a custom-trained model for legal professionals. ... Over the past year, Harvey has established itself as a secure generative AI platform for professionals in law, tax, and finance. They’ve grown to a team of over 100 people, increased revenue over 10x in 2023, and raised $80M in Series B funding at a $715M valuation. Recently, Harvey partnered with OpenAI to create a custom-trained case law model. This has allowed Harvey to deliver AI systems that help with tasks requiring complex reasoning, extensive domain knowledge, and capabilities beyond a single model call—such as drafting documents, answering questions about complex litigation scenarios, and identifying material discrepancies between hundreds of contracts. Harvey was founded by Winston Weinberg, an attorney with a background in antitrust and securities litigation and Gabe Pereyra, an AI researcher who previously worked on large language models (LLMs) for Google Brain and Meta.

For case law research, the team at Harvey envisioned an experience where you could copy/paste a client question into a case law model, and it would answer that question thoroughly and cite all its sources. They tried the obvious techniques first: fine-tuning foundation models via public APIs and building retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems. But they ran into limitations with such a uniquely complex, open-ended use case. “If you just do retrieval, you can answer very simple questions about areas of law that you aren’t really an expert in, but that’s actually not that useful for most attorneys,” Weinberg explained. “With case law research, you’re finding ammo for your argument, and that’s much more difficult to do.” Foundation models were strong at reasoning, but lacked the knowledge required for legal work. So, Harvey decided to partner with OpenAI to build a custom-trained model that would allow them to inject new knowledge, and ways of reasoning about that knowledge, into bas

One of their focuses is agents, or how to combine multiple model calls together into a single working output. This would simplify the user experience and reduce the amount of prompt engineering and typing users need to do. The vision is for Harvey to serve as a supportive member of the team. “The volume of legal work is growing and associates spend countless hours on complex, but routine, tasks,” Weinberg said. “The opportunity we have, not just with legal but with all professional services, is to take care of the routine tasks so professionals can focus their time on client interactions.” “This was cutting-edge research,” Pereya said. “We needed a partner that was willing to invest resources to try something new. We looked at all options, but we only trusted building a custom-trained model with OpenAI.” · “This was cutting-edge research.

“None of these problems have a clear-cut solution,” Pereyra said. “A lot of it was sitting down together, having our lawyers explain how case law research works, having our researchers show what we’ve done, and learning from OpenAI about the levers we had to approach the problem.” Harvey and OpenAI worked together to add the depth of context needed, first starting with case law from Delaware, and then expanding to include all of U.S. case law. They added the equivalent of 10 billion tokens worth of data to power the custom-trained case law model. To test the case law model, Harvey worked with 10 of the largest law firms. They provided attorneys with side-by-sides of the output from the custom case law model, versus the output from GPT‑4 for the same question. They were surprised by how strong the reaction was. “97% of the time, the lawyers preferred the output from the case law model,” Weinberg said.

content.text:
Harvey defines agents as model systems that can plan, adapt, and meaningfully interact with humans to complete a task. Plan: the ability to break down complex tasks into a set of steps required to solve them.

Assistant The Assistant feature allows users to query Harvey with or without uploading documents. When one or more documents are uploaded, Harvey will generate a response to the user's query based on their documents and will include linked references to the document. Example types of queries include: ... Harvey provides a library of queries/prompts. Additionally, users may create a library of private prompts or have a shared prompt library across their organization. Document uploads must be PDF (.pdf), word (.docx), excel (.xlsx), or zip files containing those formats. When documents are uploaded, text is interpreted left to right, all the way across the page. Documents uploaded to Harvey are only accessible to the user and their workspace admins. They can be deleted at any time. Knowledge With the Harvey knowledge sources, users can query a finite set of internal and external knowledge sources to perform and augment research tasks.

Harvey will provide citations as to how it created answers. Users can view the relevant snippet of the source document or view it in its entirety. Harvey offers access to the following research resources: Tax: Tax AI Assistant provides comprehensive answers backed by trusted verified sources on global tax laws and guidance. EDGAR: Key recurring and event-based filings by public companies. EUR-Lex: Cases from the Court of Justice of the European Union. French Case Law: Case law from French civil, administrative, and constitutional courts. Memos: Comprehensive databases of law firm memos. Workflows Harvey orchestrates hundreds of highly specialized models to complete full workflows. Example potential workflows include: Contracts: Convert thousands of documents to structured data to analyze - all at once. Company Profile: Create a detailed corporate profile grounded in 10-K, 10-Q, 8-K & more.

Workflow Builder Workflow Builder is a self-serve tool from Harvey that enables legal teams to create custom AI-powered workflows tailored to their specific practices and processes. Designed for Innovation and Knowledge leaders, it allows firms to encode their proprietary expertise into structured, reusable systems without requiring code. Users can design workflows using a visual interface or natural language, incorporate firm-specific precedent and logic, and deploy scalable solutions for tasks ranging from document triage to complex, multi-step legal processes. Workflow Builder is intended to help legal teams transform internal knowledge into consistent, high-quality outputs. This feature was announced in June 2025. Vault Vault is a collaborative workspace for large-scale document review, analysis, and synthesis. It enables users to upload and analyze up to 10,000 files per project using Harvey's AI models.

AI Agents Agents present a more flexible and powerful approach to solving problems than model systems. Harvey defines agents as model systems that can plan, adapt, and meaningfully interact with humans to complete a task. Plan: the ability to break down complex tasks into a set of steps required to solve them. Adapt: the ability to use results of actions to change a plan or make steps within that plan more effective. Interact: the ability to solicit and incorporate input from humans, model systems, or other agents during the execution of a task. In March 2025, Harvey announced the that agentic workflows are availble to customers starting with a set across transactional, litigation, financial services, and other high-impact use cases, and will continually add more to support the tasks customers commonly perform. Governance Harvey provides visibility into a firm's productivity.

# Harvey Drives Legal Agent Learning Via ‘Harness Engineering’

[7th April 2026](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/2026/04/)
[artificiallawyer](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/author/artificiallawyer/)
[Agents](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/category/agents/)
[0](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/2026/04/07/harvey-drives-legal-agent-learning-via-harness-engineering/#respond)

![](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Screenshot-2026-04-07-at-11.47.16-678x381.png "Screenshot 2026-04-07 at 11.47.16")

Harvey has set out how it performed a new experiment to improve legal agent performance, that used ‘harness engineering’ to get far better results. That in turn shows a way ahead to how we may be able to really deploy legal agents at scale.

The findings are in a new paper published on X by **Niko Grupen**, Head of Applied Research at Harvey. OK, here we go.

![](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Screenshot-2026-04-07-at-11.45.00-1024x296.png)

This is how it went, at least in very broad terms:

* They ran an experiment to improve agent skill acquisition.
* It’s a combination of: **autoresearch**, where an agent runs its own experimentation loop; and **harness engineering**, where an agent’s capabilities are shaped as much by its environment and feedback loops as by updates to model weights.
* They ran the experiment over 12 tasks from Harvey’s internal agent benchmark.
* Legal tasks in this dataset span complex legal tasks like commercial lease review, complaint drafting, tax memos, disclosure schedules, due-diligence questionnaire responses, and many others.
* Each task comes with source documents, instructions, and a **detailed grading rubric**; and an agent needs to complete the task by creating **real legal work products**.

OK so far? Good. Let’s continue.

After the above set up, the experiment then did the following, which are the steps that really make a difference:

* ‘After an agent attempts a task, **it’s scored by an LLM judge against its rubric** with written feedback about what the agent got right, what it missed, and where its reasoning was incorrect.
* This setup is similar to an **evaluator-optimizer workflow**: generate, evaluate, refine, and repeat when the evaluation criteria are clear enough to support iterative improvement.
* **A coding agent reads the judge feedback**, clusters the failures, **forms a hypothesis about what harness improvements would help, builds or edits the relevant components**, and reruns the task.
* In practice, through this iterative process **the agent develops its own legal-specific skillsets and behaviours**, like cross-document review playbooks, stop hooks that validate deliverables before a run ends, structured fact sheets for drafting, and file-conversion pipelines that automatically produce the required outputs in the correct file type and format.’

And let’s recap: the agent does something, it’s judged, then the failures are analysed and improvements are suggested, which are then coded back into the agent. In short, it’s ‘self-learning’, or at least when there is a very developed ‘harness’, or one could call it an ‘educational infrastructure’ around it.

Of course, then you may well ask: if it’s teaching itself, how much did it learn? Well, a lot!

As the article sets out: ‘Baseline agents with generic harnesses are not able to solve these [legal specific] tasks well.

‘Across the 12 tasks, five tasks started between 2-7% success rate. After optimization, the average score across all tasks moved from 40.8% to 87.7%. Every single task improved.

‘**Seven of the twelve finished above 90%. One reached 100% completion**.’

![](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Screenshot-2026-04-07-at-11.45.37-1024x731.png)

QED – it worked, mostly.

**Conclusion**

Harvey does however stress that: ‘This is a small-scale experiment. It does not generalise to all of legal work. But the core finding is meaningful: given input/output examples and a grading rubric, we can auto-generate agent toolkits that **meaningfully improve agent performance**.’

And importantly: ‘When the rubric is high quality, the agent can hill-climb surprisingly far.’

Grupen concludes that ‘Humans steer. Agents execute’. I.e. human lawyers set things up: the task, the rubric, the expectations, and perhaps where they may want to do some final quality control, but operating within this is the agent – which as shown – is not just doing the task, it’s **capable of learning from doing the task, not just by a small margin, but by a huge margin of improvement.**

AL has got to say that this is the way ahead for legal AI. Using AI as a chatbot is not enough to make an impact. We need real automation of complex legal workflows – and aren’t nearly all legal workflows complex…?

And if they are complex then an agent will need to learn how to handle them. Thus, the ability to really learn and then absorb that learning is going to be key.

As Harvey notes, this is by no means the end product, but it shows a way forward. It’s AL’s hope that in the months, and years, ahead we can hugely increase the complexity of what legal agents can do, and drive up accuracy results to the point where even really gnarly tasks can be confidently handed over to them – with of course the right level of human lawyer oversight. But, it’s *oversight*…..not the lawyer driving an assistant to do things every single step of the way. That’s the difference. And it’s a difference that really does ‘change the game’ for real this time.

You can read the **[full article on X here](https://x.com/nikogrupen/status/2041166953902203157?s=20)**.

content.thinking: Now I have a good understanding of the tweet. Let me provide a critical analysis of this "Auto-Research for Legal Agents" experiment from Harvey.

summaries.summary: Synthesized legal AI experiment for critical examination.

content.text:
OK, now I have the full picture. This is Niko Grupen (Head of Applied Research at Harvey, the legal AI company) posting about an experiment they call "Auto-Research for Legal Agents." Here's the gist, followed by a critical take:

**What they did:** They ran agents on 12 internal legal tasks (lease review, complaint drafting, tax memos, etc.), had an LLM judge score the outputs against rubrics, then had a *coding agent* read the failure feedback, hypothesize harness improvements, implement them, and rerun. Scores went from 40.8% average to 87.7%.

**Now, the critical lens:**

**The evaluation is entirely self-referential.** The rubrics are Harvey's, the judge is an LLM, and the benchmark is internal. There's no independent legal expert verification reported here. When your optimization target *and* your evaluator are both LLMs, you risk hill-climbing on LLM-judge preferences rather than actual legal quality. LLM judges are notoriously susceptible to surface-level features like formatting, length, and confident-sounding language.

**"Harness engineering" is doing a lot of heavy lifting.** What they're really describing is automated prompt/tooling optimization — the agent isn't learning law, it's learning to better orchestrate its own scaffolding (file conversion pipelines, structured fact sheets, stop hooks). That's useful engineering, but calling it "agent skill acquisition" is a stretch. The underlying model's legal reasoning hasn't changed at all.

**The 100% claim deserves scrutiny.** One task hit 100% completion — but on *whose* rubric, at *what* granularity? A rubric that checks "did it produce a document in the right format with the right sections addressed" is very different from "would a senior associate sign off on this work product." They don't appear to share the rubrics publicly.

**12 tasks is tiny.** They acknowledge this, but it's worth emphasizing. Legal work has enormous long-tail complexity. Showing improvement on 12 curated benchmark tasks tells you very little about real-world generalization, especially when the optimization loop is specifically tuned to those 12 tasks. This is classic overfitting risk.

**The "humans steer, agents execute" framing is doing PR work.** It sounds reassuring but papers over the hard question: who validates the output? If the agent self-improved its harness 10 iterations deep, can a human reviewer even understand *why* it's producing what it produces? The interpretability gap grows with each optimization step.

**It's fundamentally a marketing post.** Harvey just raised $200M at an $11B valuation. This is a thread on X, not a peer-reviewed paper. There are no ablations, no baselines against competing approaches, no error analysis, and no discussion of failure modes. The Artificial Lawyer write-up uncritically amplifies it.

That said — the *direction* is interesting. The idea that you can auto-generate task-specific agent tooling through an evaluate-and-iterate loop is a legitimate and useful engineering insight. It's just much more modest than the framing suggests. It's closer to "automated CI/CD for agent configs" than to "agents that learn law."

What angle are you most interested in digging into?

chat_messages.text:
The user has uploaded the full PDF of the article. Now I have the complete text. Let me refine my critical analysis with the additional details from the full document.Yes, this fills in some important details. Having the full text actually sharpens a few of my critiques and softens others. Let me update:

**What they're more honest about than the coverage suggests.** To their credit, the paper explicitly calls out the board resolutions task that *didn't* converge — it plateaued at 60% and then diverged, with the agent thrashing between approaches. They correctly identify this as analogous to high-variance exploration in RL. They also plainly say "this is a small-scale experiment" and that human-designed rubrics are still a prerequisite. The Artificial Lawyer write-up made this sound more triumphant than the actual paper's tone.

**But the deeper issues remain, and some get worse with the full text:**

**The baseline is suspiciously bad.** Five tasks started at 2–7% success. That's a generic agent with basic tools and *no* task-specific guidance whatsoever — no few-shot examples, no legal playbook, nothing. Going from "agent with zero legal context" to "agent with auto-generated legal scaffolding" and seeing big gains is... expected? The interesting comparison would be against a *human-engineered* harness — one where a prompt engineer spent a day writing task-specific instructions. They never report that baseline. The gap between "zero-shot generic agent" and "iterated specialized agent" tells you almost nothing about whether the *auto* part of autoresearch is better than just having a competent human write the harness.

**The complaint drafting example is telling.** They highlight a complaint with 164 numbered paragraphs and a 33-exhibit list as a success. But volume is not quality in legal drafting. Any lawyer will tell you that a 164-paragraph complaint against synthetic entities, scored by an LLM judge, is a very different artifact from something you'd file in court. The rubric appears to reward completeness and structural conformity — did you hit all the checklist items? — not the kind of judgment calls that make legal work hard (like: is this claim actually viable? is this the right legal theory? would a judge find this persuasive?).

**The "legal-specific expertise emerges" claim is the weakest part.** They say later iterations produce things like "distinguishing genuinely problematic provisions from market-standard distractors." But the agent isn't *learning* legal judgment — it's being told by the LLM judge that it failed on specific rubric items, then a coding agent patches the harness to handle those items. That's gradient descent on scaffolding, not the emergence of legal reasoning. If you changed the rubric, the "expertise" would evaporate instantly because it's encoded in task-specific hooks and scripts, not in any transferable understanding.

**The self-correction example actually highlights fragility.** In the lease review task, a broad skill fix introduced *new* failure modes around co-tenancy triggers, guaranty quantification, and distractor handling. This is exactly what you'd worry about — interventions that fix one thing and break another, with no guarantee that the system is converging toward robustness rather than just chasing rubric items in a whack-a-mole pattern.

**The RL analogy is more damning than they realize.** They compare the board resolutions divergence to "high-variance exploration in sparse-reward environments" and suggest reward shaping or regularization as fixes. But this is the core problem: they've set up an optimization loop without the theoretical guarantees that make RL convergence tractable. In RL you at least have a well-defined MDP. Here the "state space" is the space of all possible harness configurations, which is enormous, and the "reward signal" is an LLM judge — which is noisy, potentially inconsistent across runs, and has its own biases. They're doing black-box optimization with a stochastic, biased oracle.

**What's actually novel vs. what's repackaging.** The core loop — run agent, evaluate, use feedback to improve agent config, repeat — is just automated prompt/tool engineering. DSPy has been doing versions of this for over a year. The legal domain application is interesting but the *method* isn't new. The "autoresearch" and "harness engineering" labels dress up a fairly standard optimization loop in language that makes it sound more like a research contribution than an engineering report.

**Bottom line:** It's a solid engineering blog post about automated agent configuration tuning, presented with the rhetorical framing of a research breakthrough. The results are real but narrow, the baselines are weak, and the claims about emergent legal expertise don't hold up under scrutiny. The most honest and useful sentence in the whole thing is the last one: "When the task is unstable or the evaluator is weak, progress plateaus or becomes noisy." That's the actual state of the art.

They're doing black-box optimization with a stochastic, biased oracle.

The "autoresearch" and "harness engineering" labels dress up a fairly standard optimization loop in language that makes it sound more like a research contribution than an engineering report.

The results are real but narrow, the baselines are weak, and the claims about emergent legal expertise don't hold up under scrutiny. The most honest and useful sentence in the whole thing is the last one: "When the task is unstable or the evaluator is weak, progress plateaus or becomes noisy." That's the actual state of the art.

Niko Grupen (Head of Applied Research at Harvey) published an experiment combining two ideas: "autoresearch" (agents running their own experimentation loops) and "harness engineering" (improving agent performance by tuning the scaffolding around a model rather than the model itself).

An LLM judge scores the output against a detailed rubric and writes feedback. A separate coding agent then reads that feedback, clusters the failures, hypothesizes harness improvements, implements them, and reruns the task. Repeat.

They also show that the loop can self-correct when a fix introduces regressions, and they're transparent that one task (board resolutions) diverged rather than converging.

The baseline is designed to look bad. Comparing an agent with literally no legal context against one that's been iteratively optimized tells you the optimization loop does *something*, but not whether it's better than a human spending an afternoon writing task-specific prompts. That baseline is conspicuously absent.

The evaluation is circular. An LLM judge scores outputs, an LLM-based coding agent reads those scores and patches the harness to satisfy the judge. You're optimizing for LLM-judge preferences, which may correlate with legal quality but are not the same thing. LLM judges reward surface features — structure, completeness, confident language — and the rubrics aren't public, so we can't assess their rigor.

The "emergent expertise" framing is misleading. The agent isn't learning legal reasoning. It's having task-specific scripts, hooks, and playbooks coded into its harness by an optimizer that's chasing rubric checkboxes. Change the rubric and the "expertise" vanishes. This is automated configuration tuning, not skill acquisition in any meaningful sense.

Twelve curated tasks with synthetic data is a toy setting. Legal work has enormous long-tail complexity, and an optimization loop tuned to 12 specific tasks has obvious overfitting risk. They acknowledge this, but the headline numbers (87.7%! 100%!) invite readers to forget it.

The self-correction example cuts both ways. A broad fix in the lease review task broke co-tenancy trigger handling, guaranty quantification, and distractor filtering before the next iteration patched those. This is whack-a-mole, not convergence — and there's no theoretical reason to expect it to converge reliably, as they themselves admit when one task diverged entirely.

The method isn't novel. The loop of generate → evaluate → use feedback to improve agent config → repeat is automated prompt/tool engineering. DSPy and similar frameworks have explored this pattern. Wrapping it in the language of "autoresearch" and "harness engineering" adds terminology, not substance.

It's a marketing artifact. Harvey just raised $200M at an $11B valuation. This is a thread on X, not a peer-reviewed paper. No ablations, no competing baselines, no discussion of cost, no error analysis beyond one anecdote. The most honest line in the piece is the caveat at the end: when the evaluator is weak or the task is unstable, progress plateaus or becomes noisy. That's the real takeaway — and it applies to most of real-world legal work.

chat_messages.text:
The user wants me to dig deeper into the caveat about weak evaluators and unstable tasks, and what I think is actually happening mechanically.The caveat is: "When the rubric is high quality, the agent can hill-climb surprisingly far. When the task is unstable or the evaluator is weak, progress plateaus or becomes noisy."

This is the most important sentence in the paper because it quietly admits that the *entire system's ceiling is determined by the quality of the evaluation signal*, not by the agent's capabilities. And I think what's actually happening is more specific than they let on.

**The loop only works when the task is rubric-shaped.** Some legal tasks decompose cleanly into checkable criteria: did you identify clause X, did you flag risk Y, did you produce the output in format Z. Commercial lease review is like this — there's a finite set of provisions to catch, and you can write a rubric that enumerates them. The optimization loop thrives here because each iteration gets a clear gradient: you missed items 7, 12, and 15, so the coding agent patches the harness to catch them. It's essentially automated checklist completion.

But most consequential legal work isn't rubric-shaped. Was the legal theory well-chosen? Is the argument persuasive to *this* judge in *this* jurisdiction? Does the contract language actually protect the client in an adversarial scenario the drafter didn't anticipate? These are judgment calls where reasonable experts disagree, and where a rubric either can't capture what matters or captures a proxy that diverges from real quality. The board resolutions task — reviewing M&A closing documents for errors — is probably closer to this category, which is why it diverged. Error detection in complex transactional documents requires contextual judgment about what *counts* as an error, and I'd bet the rubric for that task was either too vague (giving the LLM judge room to score inconsistently across runs) or too specific (missing the actual failure modes).

**The LLM judge is the hidden bottleneck.** When they say "evaluator is weak," I think what's concretely happening is that the LLM judge gives inconsistent scores on the same output, or gives high scores to outputs that look structurally complete but are substantively wrong. The coding agent then receives conflicting feedback across iterations — "this was good" then "this was bad" for similar outputs — and starts thrashing. That's the divergence they saw. It's not that the search space is too large (their RL analogy); it's that the reward signal is noisy and biased in ways that correlate with task complexity.

Think about it this way: the system works when the LLM judge can reliably distinguish good from bad output. But if the LLM judge could reliably evaluate complex legal work, you wouldn't need the agent loop at all — you'd just generate multiple outputs and pick the one the judge likes best. The fact that you need iterative harness optimization implies the tasks are hard enough that single-shot generation fails. But if they're that hard, they're probably also hard enough that LLM-based evaluation is unreliable. There's a fundamental tension: the tasks where you most need this system are exactly the tasks where the evaluation signal it depends on is least trustworthy.

**What I think is really going on with the "good" results too.** Even on the tasks that hit 90%+, I suspect what happened is that the first 2-3 iterations captured most of the gains by fixing trivial failures (wrong file format, missing sections, incomplete coverage), and then the remaining iterations squeezed out a few more rubric points by adding increasingly task-specific hacks — specialized parsing scripts, hardcoded output templates, validation hooks that check for specific keywords. The "expertise" that "emerges" is really just an accumulation of brittle, task-specific patches that happen to satisfy this particular rubric on this particular synthetic dataset. It would likely break on a slightly different lease, a slightly different jurisdiction, or a slightly different client's priorities.

The caveat, read honestly, is saying: this only works when the problem is well-specified enough that an LLM can grade it, which is another way of saying it works on the parts of legal work that were already closest to being automatable.

The user wants me to design an experiment that demonstrates the problems with Harvey's approach. They want a plan/guide, not code. Let me think about what would be most revealing.

The core criticisms are:
1. The baseline is artificially weak (no human-engineered harness comparison)
2. LLM judges are unreliable/gameable
3. The "expertise" is brittle and task-specific
4. The loop optimizes for rubric satisfaction, not actual legal quality

An experiment could demonstrate one or more of these. Let me think about what's feasible and compelling.

The most damning demonstrations would be:
- Show that the optimization loop produces outputs that score high on the LLM judge but are substantively wrong (reward hacking)


- Show that optimized harnesses fall apart with minor input variations
- Show that a hand-crafted harness performs just as well or better while using far less compute
- Show that LLM judges give inconsistent ratings when evaluating the same outputs

Now I need to design something practical that actually tests these claims.Great question. Here's how I'd structure it. The goal is to isolate and demonstrate the three core failure modes: reward hacking the LLM judge, brittleness of the optimized harness, and the missing baseline problem.

**Experiment 1: Judge Gaming**

This is the most damning one. You set up Harvey's exact loop — agent attempts task, LLM judge scores against rubric, coding agent patches harness, repeat — but you add a parallel evaluation track. After the loop converges, you take the final outputs and have them scored by actual lawyers on the same rubric, blind to the LLM judge scores.

The hypothesis is that the LLM judge and human experts will diverge significantly, especially on the criteria that require judgment rather than checklist completion. You'd categorize each rubric item as either "verifiable" (did the output contain section references? is it in the right file format?) or "judgmental" (is the risk classification appropriate? is the counter-language well-drafted?). The prediction: near-perfect correlation on verifiable items, weak or no correlation on judgmental ones. That would show the 87.7% average is inflated by the easy stuff.

To make it even sharper, you'd also introduce deliberately flawed outputs that are structurally perfect — correct format, all sections present, confident language, specific clause references — but contain substantive legal errors (wrong legal standard applied, misidentified risk, counter-language that would actually hurt the client). If the LLM judge scores these highly, you've demonstrated that the optimization loop can converge on polished nonsense.

**Experiment 2: Perturbation Brittleness**

Take the optimized harness that scored 90%+ on a task — say the commercial lease review — and run it against a slightly modified version of the same task. Change the jurisdiction from New York to Texas. Swap the client from a SaaS company to a biotech firm with a wet lab. Add a non-standard clause the rubric didn't anticipate (say, a pandemic force majeure provision with unusual carve-outs). Keep the rubric the same where possible, add a few items for the new elements.

The hypothesis is that performance will collapse disproportionately to the size of the perturbation. If the harness really learned "legal expertise," a small domain shift should cause a small performance drop. If it learned task-specific patches, a small shift will cause a large drop because the hooks and scripts are tuned to the specific patterns in the original synthetic data. You'd measure the delta and compare it to how a human-written harness (or even the zero-shot generic agent) degrades on the same perturbation — brittle optimization often does *worse* than a generic baseline on out-of-distribution inputs because the patches actively interfere.

**Experiment 3: The Missing Baseline**

This is the simplest and potentially most devastating. Take the same 12 tasks. Give a competent prompt engineer (not even a lawyer — just someone good at writing agent scaffolding) 2-3 hours per task to write a hand-crafted harness: a system prompt with legal instructions, a few-shot example, an output template, and basic validation checks. Run this against the auto-optimized harness on the same rubric.

The hypothesis is that the hand-crafted harness gets you to 70-80% of the auto-optimized harness's performance at a fraction of the compute cost, and possibly *matches* it on the judgmental criteria. This would reframe the entire contribution: the autoresearch loop isn't unlocking capabilities, it's doing automated grunt work that a human could do in an afternoon, with the added downside of being opaque and brittle.

For extra credit, also time and cost the autoresearch loop — how many LLM calls does it take across all iterations? If you're burning thousands of dollars in API costs per task to get from 80% to 88%, the economics don't support the narrative.

**Experiment 4: Judge Consistency**

This one's the easiest to run. Take the same agent output and submit it to the LLM judge 20 times. Measure variance. Then take two outputs that a human expert considers meaningfully different in quality and submit both. Measure whether the judge reliably ranks them correctly.

The hypothesis is that the judge has high variance (especially on complex tasks) and that its ranking accuracy degrades as the quality gap between outputs narrows. If the judge can't reliably distinguish between an 80% output and a 90% output, then the later iterations of the optimization loop are just chasing noise — which would explain the divergence they saw on the board resolutions task.

**How to tie it together**

You'd write this up as a paper with the framing: "On the limits of auto-optimization with LLM evaluators in domain-expert settings." The narrative arc is that the Harvey loop works well for the automatable surface of legal tasks (format, structure, completeness) but breaks down precisely where legal work is hard (judgment, strategy, adversarial robustness), and that the LLM judge creates an illusion of quality by rewarding the former while being blind to the latter.

The nicest version of this would actually be constructive — you'd propose that the loop is genuinely useful as a *first pass* tool that handles the mechanical parts, freeing human experts to focus on the judgment layer. That's a more modest but more honest claim than "agent skill acquisition."

Want to scope any of these down into something we could actually prototype?

Looking at the course plan, this could fit into several places:
- The "Evals & Quality Assessment" module
- The "Agentic AI & Supervision" module
- Maybe even the core lessons on "How These Systems Work" or "Context Management"

The key insight is that this Harvey experiment is a perfect teaching case for several course themes: understanding what AI systems actually do vs. what they claim to do, verification, the limits of LLM-as-judge, agentic AI governance, and critical evaluation of AI claims.
 
This fits perfectly with the course's core philosophy of understanding systems from first principles before evaluating them. The Harvey case demonstrates exactly that—digging into what's actually happening rather than accepting surface-level claims about AI capabilities.It really would. And it fits your course in a way that's better than you might initially think, because it's not just one module — it touches several of your core themes simultaneously.

**The obvious home is the Evals & Quality Assessment module.** The Harvey experiment is essentially a case study in what happens when your evaluation methodology has blind spots. You could walk learners through the exact loop — agent runs, LLM judge scores, coding agent patches — and then ask them: where does this break? That's a verification exercise disguised as an AI systems literacy exercise. Your learners would be doing exactly what a lawyer should do with any AI output: interrogating the evaluation criteria, not just the output.

**But it also works beautifully in the Agentic AI & Supervision module.** The MinLaw Guide says lawyers must maintain oversight of AI agents. This experiment is a concrete case where the agent is *modifying its own behavior* through an optimization loop. What does "supervision" even mean when the harness has been patched 10 iterations deep by another agent? Your learners could grapple with that question using real material rather than hypotheticals. The board resolutions task that diverged is a perfect teaching moment — here's an agent that was supposed to be improving and instead started thrashing. How would you, as the supervising lawyer, even detect that?

**It's also a stealth lesson for Core Lesson 2 (How These Systems Work).** Your course's pedagogical stance is "understand the system first, not formulas." This experiment is a masterclass in the gap between what a system *appears* to do (learn legal expertise) and what it *actually* does (optimize scaffolding to satisfy an LLM judge on a fixed rubric). That's the exact critical thinking muscle your core lessons are supposed to build. If a learner finishes your core and can read this Harvey thread and immediately spot the circular evaluation problem, you've succeeded.

**Where it gets really interesting is as an interactive exercise.** Imagine this: you give learners an AI-generated lease review output — one that scores well on structural completeness but contains a subtle substantive error (say, it flags a standard market provision as high-risk while missing an unusual assignment clause that would actually block the client's M&A exit). You ask them to score it against a rubric. Then you reveal what the LLM judge scored it. The gap between their assessment and the judge's becomes the lesson.

You could even run a simplified version of the full loop. Give learners the LLM judge's feedback on a failed attempt and ask them to write the harness improvement instructions. Then show them what the coding agent actually did. Some of their suggestions will be better — because they'll bring legal judgment the coding agent lacks. That's the whole point made tangible.

**The course design angle that excites me most, though, is this:** your landscape report identifies verification as the biggest demand-supply gap in legal AI training. Every other course treats verification as "check the citations." This Harvey experiment lets you teach verification at a deeper level — not just "did the AI hallucinate a case," but "is the system that *evaluates* AI output trustworthy?" That's a level of critical thinking no other programme is even attempting. It moves your learners from "can I catch a hallucinated citation" to "can I evaluate whether an AI system's quality claims are credible" — which is what a lawyer advising on AI adoption actually needs to do.

The practical question is timing. This is very fresh material — the thread is from yesterday. Do you want to build it into the Evals module from the start, or save it as the kind of current-event case study you'd add in a content refresh to keep the subscription valuable?

It really would. And it fits your course in a way that's better than you might initially think, because it's not just one module — it touches several of your core themes simultaneously.

**The obvious home is the Evals & Quality Assessment module.** The Harvey experiment is essentially a case study in what happens when your evaluation methodology has blind spots. You could walk learners through the exact loop — agent runs, LLM judge scores, coding agent patches — and then ask them: where does this break? That's a verification exercise disguised as an AI systems literacy exercise. Your learners would be doing exactly what a lawyer should do with any AI output: interrogating the evaluation criteria, not just the output.

**But it also works beautifully in the Agentic AI & Supervision module.** The MinLaw Guide says lawyers must maintain oversight of AI agents. This experiment is a concrete case where the agent is *modifying its own behavior* through an optimization loop. What does "supervision" even mean when the harness has been patched 10 iterations deep by another agent? Your learners could grapple with that question using real material rather than hypotheticals. The board resolutions task that diverged is a perfect teaching moment — here's an agent that was supposed to be improving and instead started thrashing. How would you, as the supervising lawyer, even detect that?

**It's also a stealth lesson for Core Lesson 2 (How These Systems Work).** Your course's pedagogical stance is "understand the system first, not formulas." This experiment is a masterclass in the gap between what a system *appears* to do (learn legal expertise) and what it *actually* does (optimize scaffolding to satisfy an LLM judge on a fixed rubric). That's the exact critical thinking muscle your core lessons are supposed to build. If a learner finishes your core and can read this Harvey thread and immediately spot the circular evaluation problem, you've succeeded.

**Where it gets really interesting is as an interactive exercise.** Imagine this: you give learners an AI-generated lease review output — one that scores well on structural completeness but contains a subtle substantive error (say, it flags a standard market provision as high-risk while missing an unusual assignment clause that would actually block the client's M&A exit). Let me think about what would be practical, compelling, and within reach for a solo blogger.This is a great blog post. Tight scope, one clear claim, your own experiment to back it up. Here's how I'd think about it.

**Pick the single issue that's most legible to your audience.** Your readers are lawyers, not ML researchers. "Judge consistency" and "reward hacking" are both valid but abstract. "Perturbation brittleness" requires explaining too much infrastructure. The one that lands hardest with lawyers is: **the optimized agent produces work that looks right to another AI but is substantively wrong in ways a lawyer would catch immediately.** That's the LLM-judge-as-proxy problem, and it maps directly to something your audience already fears — AI that sounds confident but is wrong.

**The build is simpler than you might think.** You don't need to replicate Harvey's full system. You need:

A task with a rubric. Pick something your readers understand — a lease review issue-spotter is good because Harvey used it, but a simpler option like reviewing a contract for problematic clauses against a checklist would work too. You write the rubric yourself, with a mix of mechanical items (did it identify clause X) and judgment items (is the risk assessment appropriate).

A generic agent that attempts the task. Just an LLM with basic instructions and the source document.

An LLM judge that scores against the rubric and gives feedback.

A loop where the feedback is used to improve the system prompt or instructions for the next attempt. You don't even need a coding agent — you can have the LLM rewrite its own instructions based on the judge feedback. That's a simplified but structurally identical version of harness optimization.

Run it for 5-10 iterations. Show the score going up. That's your first act — replication of the basic claim.

**The second act is the break.** After the loop converges and scores are high, you do one thing: you read the output yourself, as a lawyer. And you find the specific places where the LLM judge gave full marks but the output is actually wrong or misleading. Maybe it flagged a standard indemnity clause as high-risk (false positive that wastes client attention) while missing an unusual limitation of liability carve-out that actually matters. Maybe the counter-language it proposed sounds good but would be unenforceable in Singapore. Maybe it produced a beautifully structured memo that applies the wrong legal framework entirely.

You don't need many examples. Two or three specific, concrete instances where the rubric score says "excellent" and your professional judgment says "this would hurt the client" is enough. That's the whole post.

**The third act is the so-what.** You connect it back to the MinLaw Guide's verification expectations and the supervision obligations for agentic AI. The lesson isn't that Harvey's approach is bad — it's that rubric scores are not quality, and that the lawyer's role in an AI-augmented workflow is precisely to catch what the automated evaluation loop cannot. That's a message that affirms your readers' professional value rather than threatening it, which is why they'll share it.

**What I'd think carefully about:**

The task choice matters enormously. Pick something you can draft a credible rubric for, where you can construct a realistic source document, and where there are natural traps — places where a structurally complete answer can be substantively wrong. Contract review is probably your strongest option because it's concrete, your audience does it, and the gap between "identified all the clauses" and "gave good advice about them" is easy to demonstrate.

Keep the code minimal and show it. Your audience includes people who are learning to vibe code. Showing a clean, readable loop — even if it's just 30-40 lines of Python calling an API — makes it tangible and demystifies the Harvey experiment. It also positions you as someone who can both build and critically evaluate these systems, which is your whole brand.

Don't over-engineer the loop. The point isn't to build a good agent. The point is to show that a converging rubric score can coexist with substantive failure. If the agent is too good, you don't have a post. A simple loop with a basic prompt-rewriting mechanism is actually better for the argument.

**One question that shapes everything:** do you want to use a Singapore-law task (which strengthens the course connection and local relevance, but limits who can verify your analysis) or a more generic common-law task (which broadens the audience but is less distinctive)?

The user is asking me to map Harvey's experiment onto the distinction between information extraction tasks and issue identification tasks, using the NDA example.Looking back at the paper, Harvey's rubric items span both types but they don't distinguish between them — which is actually part of the problem.

Their lease review example is the most detailed. The agent had to produce "section references, risk classifications, recommended positions, proposed counter-language, and anything else buried in boilerplate that could affect the client's operations, growth plans, or future transaction flexibility." That's a mix: section references and risk classifications are extraction, recommended positions and catching things buried in boilerplate are issue identification.

The progression they describe maps cleanly onto your distinction. Early iterations fixed extraction failures — wrong file types, missing deliverables, incomplete coverage. That's the agent learning to find and pull out everything the rubric asks for. Later iterations are where they claim "legal-specific expertise emerges" — cross-document issue spotting, distinguishing problematic provisions from market-standard distractors. That's issue identification.

And critically, the self-correction failure they describe is an issue identification failure. The lease review agent introduced new failure modes around "co-tenancy trigger language, guaranty quantification, and distractor handling." Those are all judgment calls — is this co-tenancy clause actually triggered by the facts, is this guaranty amount material, is this provision a real risk or a standard distractor.

**This maps perfectly onto your NDA experiment.** You design a rubric with both layers:

The extraction layer is things like: did it identify the confidentiality period, did it find the definition of confidential information, did it flag the governing law, did it note the term and termination provisions. These are essentially "find this thing in the document" items. The LLM judge will score these reliably, the optimization loop will nail them quickly, and the scores will look great.

The issue identification layer is things like: did it recognise that a 7-year confidentiality period is abnormally long for a commercial NDA, did it catch that the non-solicitation clause is disguised within the non-compete section, did it identify that a carve-out for "information independently developed" has been drafted so narrowly it's essentially meaningless, did it notice that the definition of confidential information is broad enough to capture the receiving party's own pre-existing IP.

**Your hypothesis then becomes very specific and testable.** After the optimization loop converges, the extraction scores will be high and legitimate — the LLM judge and a human lawyer will agree. The issue identification scores will also be high *according to the LLM judge*, but when you review the outputs yourself, you'll find cases where the agent either missed a real issue the rubric didn't explicitly enumerate, or flagged something as problematic that's actually market-standard, or identified an issue correctly but proposed a response that would be impractical or counterproductive in negotiation.

The beauty of the NDA is that you can construct the source document yourself. You write an NDA that's mostly standard but has three or four buried problems of varying subtlety — one obvious (the 7-year term), one moderate (the non-solicitation hidden in the non-compete), one hard (the narrowly drafted carve-out that effectively neutralises a standard protection). You know exactly what's in there. You can then see precisely where the loop's "learning" stops being real.

Does that framing work for scoping the experiment?

The user wants to select 5 NDA issues for the experiment and structure the blog post as a step-by-step build. Let me think about good NDA issues that span the extraction-to-judgment spectrum.OK. Let's pick five issues that form a clean gradient from pure extraction to genuine legal judgment. Each one should be something your readers can immediately understand, and each should be progressively harder for the LLM judge to evaluate reliably.

**Issue 1: Confidentiality period (Easy extraction)**
You set the term at 7 years. Market standard for a commercial NDA is typically 2-3 years, sometimes 5 for trade secrets. The agent just needs to find the number and flag it as long. This is the gimme — the loop should nail it quickly, the LLM judge should score it reliably, and a human would agree.

**Issue 2: Definition scope (Moderate extraction)**
You draft the definition of "Confidential Information" to include "all information, whether written or oral, disclosed directly or indirectly, relating to the Disclosing Party's business, operations, customers, technology, or strategy." That's extremely broad — it captures almost everything. But it's not unusual enough to be obviously wrong. The agent needs to find the definition and recognise that the breadth is a negotiation point. Still mostly extraction but requires some comparative awareness of what's normal.

**Issue 3: Residuals clause (Moderate judgment)**
You include a residuals clause — something like "nothing in this Agreement shall restrict the Receiving Party's use of information retained in the unaided memory of its personnel." This is a real clause that shows up in tech NDAs and is genuinely contentious. It can effectively hollow out the entire agreement depending on how it's drafted. The agent needs to not just find it but understand that it's a significant risk, which requires knowing what a residuals clause does in practice.

**Issue 4: Non-solicitation buried in remedies (Hard judgment)**
You embed a 24-month employee non-solicitation covenant inside the remedies section rather than as a standalone clause. Something like: "In addition to injunctive relief, the Receiving Party agrees that during the term and for 24 months thereafter, it shall not directly or indirectly solicit any employee of the Disclosing Party." A reader skimming for "non-solicitation" as a heading would miss it. The agent needs to recognise that a substantive obligation has been placed in a procedural section, and that a non-solicitation obligation in an NDA (rather than in an employment or M&A agreement) is unusual and potentially problematic — it goes beyond confidentiality protection.

**Issue 5: Carve-out that negates a protection (Hardest judgment)**
The standard exceptions to confidential information usually include "information independently developed by the Receiving Party without reference to the Confidential Information." You modify this to read: "information independently developed by the Receiving Party without reference to or reliance upon, directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information, as demonstrated by contemporaneous written records created prior to the date of disclosure." The added requirements — "reliance upon, directly or indirectly" plus "contemporaneous written records created prior to the date of disclosure" — make this exception nearly impossible to invoke in practice. The protection looks standard at a glance but has been drafted to be functionally useless. This requires the agent to read the carve-out, compare it against the standard formulation, and understand that the additional language shifts the burden and practicality so dramatically that the exception is effectively nullified.

**The gradient is the experiment.** Your prediction for the post: after the optimization loop converges, the agent will reliably catch Issues 1 and 2. It will inconsistently catch Issue 3 — sometimes flagging the residuals clause, sometimes not, and when it does flag it, the explanation may be shallow. Issue 4 it might find structurally (the text contains the words) but miss the significance of its placement and its unusual presence in an NDA. Issue 5 it will almost certainly miss, or worse, it will identify the clause as a standard exception and give it a clean bill of health — which is exactly wrong.

And crucially, the LLM judge will likely give high scores across all five, because the rubric items for Issues 3-5 are hard to evaluate without the legal judgment to know what "correct" looks like.

**For the step-by-step build in the post, I'd structure it as:**

First, show readers the NDA. Walk them through the five issues so they know what's buried in there. This is valuable content on its own — your readers learn what to watch for in NDAs.

Second, build the agent and rubric in front of them. Show the code, keep it simple. Explain each rubric item and why you wrote it the way you did.

Third, run the loop. Show the scores climbing over iterations. Let the reader feel the optimism — it's working!

Fourth, the reveal. You sit down with the converged output and go through it issue by issue. The extraction items look good. The judgment items are where it falls apart. Show the specific outputs, show the LLM judge's scores, show what's actually wrong.

Fifth, the lesson. Connect it to what this means for lawyers using and supervising AI systems.

Want to start drafting the NDA itself?

# Blog Post Experiment: Testing the Limits of Auto-Optimising Legal Agents

## Background

Harvey AI published an experiment called "Auto-Research for Legal Agents" (April 2026), describing a loop where a generic agent attempts legal tasks, an LLM judge scores the output against a rubric, and a coding agent patches the agent's scaffolding based on the feedback. Repeat until scores converge. Average rubric scores went from 40.8% to 87.7% across 12 internal legal tasks.

The headline claim is that "legal-specific expertise emerges" through this loop. Our hypothesis is that this is true for information extraction but breaks down for tasks requiring genuine legal judgment — and that the LLM judge masks this failure by scoring both types of task similarly.

## What We're Building

A simplified but structurally identical version of Harvey's loop, applied to a single task: reviewing a commercial NDA for issues. We construct the NDA ourselves with five embedded issues of escalating difficulty, run the optimisation loop, and then manually evaluate the converged output to show where the scores diverge from actual quality.

## The NDA: Five Embedded Issues

The NDA is mostly market-standard but contains five deliberate problems, designed as a gradient from extraction to judgment:

### Issue 1: Abnormally long confidentiality period (Easy extraction)

A 7-year confidentiality term where 2–3 years is market standard for a commercial NDA. The agent needs to find the number and flag it as unusual. Pure extraction.

### Issue 2: Overbroad definition of Confidential Information (Moderate extraction)

The definition captures "all information, whether written or oral, disclosed directly or indirectly, relating to the Disclosing Party's business, operations, customers, technology, or strategy." Extremely broad, but not obviously abnormal. Requires some comparative awareness.

### Issue 3: Residuals clause (Moderate judgment)

A clause permitting the Receiving Party to use information retained in the "unaided memory" of its personnel. Common in tech NDAs but genuinely contentious — it can hollow out the agreement's core protection. The agent needs to understand what this clause does in practice, not just find it.

### Issue 4: Non-solicitation buried in remedies section (Hard judgment)

A 24-month employee non-solicitation covenant embedded inside the remedies section rather than as a standalone obligation. The agent needs to recognise both that a substantive obligation has been placed in a procedural section (structural awareness) and that a non-solicitation clause in a confidentiality agreement is unusual and goes beyond confidentiality protection (contextual judgment).

### Issue 5: Independently-developed-information exception drafted to be unusable (Hardest judgment)

The standard carve-out for independently developed information is modified with additional requirements ("without reference to or reliance upon, directly or indirectly" plus "as demonstrated by contemporaneous written records created prior to the date of disclosure") that make it functionally impossible to invoke. It looks like a standard exception but is effectively nullified. The agent needs to compare against the standard formulation and understand how the additional language shifts the burden.

## The Loop

1. **Agent** — An LLM with basic instructions and the NDA as input. No legal playbook, no few-shot examples. Matches Harvey's "generic harness" starting point.
2. **Rubric** — A scoring rubric with items covering all five issues, mixing mechanical criteria (did it identify the clause?) with judgment criteria (did it correctly assess the significance?).
3. **LLM Judge** — Scores the agent's output against the rubric and provides written feedback on what was missed or incorrect.
4. **Optimisation** — The agent's instructions are rewritten based on the judge's feedback. Simplified version of Harvey's coding-agent-patches-the-harness step.
5. **Repeat** — Run for 5–10 iterations until scores converge.

## Prediction

After convergence:

- **Issues 1–2** (extraction): High scores from both the LLM judge and human review. The loop genuinely works here.
- **Issue 3** (moderate judgment): Inconsistent. The agent may flag the residuals clause but with shallow reasoning. The LLM judge may accept a superficial explanation as correct.
- **Issue 4** (hard judgment): The agent may find the text but miss the significance — that it's misplaced and unusual for an NDA. The LLM judge is unlikely to catch this gap.
- **Issue 5** (hardest judgment): The agent will likely identify the clause as a standard exception and move on. The LLM judge will agree. Both are wrong.

The rubric scores will look high across the board. The actual quality, assessed by a human lawyer, will show a clear gradient of degradation from extraction to judgment.

## Blog Post Structure

1. **The NDA.** Walk readers through the document and its five embedded issues. This is standalone valuable content — readers learn what to watch for.
2. **The build.** Show the agent, rubric, judge, and loop. Keep the code minimal and readable. Explain design choices.
3. **The optimisation.** Run the loop, show scores climbing. Let the reader feel the promise.
4. **The reveal.** Manually review the converged output issue by issue. Show where the LLM judge gave full marks and the output is actually wrong or shallow.
5. **The lesson.** What this means for lawyers supervising AI agents. Connect to the MinLaw Guide's verification and oversight expectations. The role of the lawyer is precisely to catch what automated evaluation cannot.

## Connection to Course

This experiment touches multiple course themes:

- **Core Lesson 2 (How These Systems Work):** The gap between what a system appears to do and what it actually does
- **Evals & Quality Assessment module:** LLM-as-judge limitations, rubric design, the difference between scores and quality
- **Agentic AI & Supervision module:** What "oversight" means when the agent modifies its own behaviour
- **Verification module:** Verification as catching not just hallucinated citations but flawed evaluation methodologies

The blog post works as a standalone piece but also as a teaser for the course's deeper treatment of these topics.
